title: Phase 4.5-4.6: Verify TOCTOU mitigations and full pipeline
description:
Verify TOCTOU analysis and end-to-end pipeline.

## 4.5 TOCTOU mitigations:
- [ ] pidfd_open() immediately after SO_PEERCRED verification (Linux 5.3+)
- [ ] memfd_create for secret injection (no filesystem path to race)
- [ ] Fallback for older kernels: /proc/<pid>/exe symlink verification
- [ ] macOS: LOCAL_PEERPID + session token authentication
- [ ] PreToolUse hook → command execution: NOT VULNERABLE (check IS execution path)
- [ ] Bwrap sandbox setup: NOT VULNERABLE (clone() with namespace flags is atomic)

## 4.6 Full execution pipeline:
- [ ] Wire everything together: parse → resolve → sandbox → execute → scrub → return
- [ ] Error handling:
  - If sigild unreachable: fail loudly with clear error (no silent passthrough)
  - If placeholder cannot resolve: fail with error listing missing path
  - If sandbox creation fails: fall back to hook-only mode with warning

## Red team tests:
- From inside sandbox: attempt ptrace (should fail via seccomp)
- From inside sandbox: attempt /proc/<sigild_pid>/mem (should fail via PID namespace)
- From inside sandbox: modify PATH or LD_PRELOAD (should be blocked)
- Verify sandbox overhead < 30ms with cached secrets
- Test with real Claude Code Bash tool calls end-to-end

## Acceptance
- All TOCTOU surfaces have mitigations
- Full pipeline works end-to-end
- Sandbox overhead is < 30ms

title: Phase 3.1: Verify command parser with all injection modes
description:

## Five injection modes to verify:

title: Phase 2.5-2.7: Verify audit lifecycle, IPC protocol, signal handling
description:
Verify audit rotation, IPC protocol, and signal handling.

## 2.5 Audit log lifecycle:
- [ ] Size-based rotation: rotate when audit.jsonl exceeds max_size (default 50MB)
- [ ] Rotation preserves hash-chain continuity across files
- [ ] Compress rotated logs if compress=true
- [ ] sigil audit export --from/--to --format json|csv
- [ ] sigil audit verify checks hash chain integrity
- [ ] sigil audit prune removes logs exceeding retention
- [ ] sigil audit stats shows log size, entry count, date range
- [ ] Tamper detection on startup: refuse start if chain broken (unless --force)

## 2.6 IPC protocol:
- [ ] Length-prefixed JSON over Unix socket
- [ ] Request envelope: v, id, op, token, payload
- [ ] Response envelope: v, id, ok, payload/error
- [ ] All 15 error codes implemented
- [ ] Multiplexed requests with request ID correlation
- [ ] Streaming protocol for long-running operations
- [ ] Protocol version field enables backward compatibility

## 2.7 Signal handling:
- [ ] SIGTERM/SIGINT: graceful shutdown with 5s drain
- [ ] SIGHUP: reload config (no vault re-unseal)
- [ ] SIGUSR1: dump status to audit log
- [ ] SIGUSR2: force audit log rotation
- [ ] SIGQUIT: immediate exit (debugging only)
- [ ] SIGPIPE: ignored (handled per-connection)
- [ ] sigil-shell forwards signals to sandbox child
- [ ] PR_SET_PDEATHSIG(SIGKILL) on sandbox child

## Tests:
- Fill audit log past 50MB, verify rotation occurs
- Send multiplexed requests, verify responses correlate by ID
- Run sigil audit verify, confirm chain integrity
- Send SIGTERM to daemon, verify graceful shutdown
- Forward SIGINT to sandbox child, verify it receives signal

## Acceptance
- Audit log rotation preserves hash chain
- IPC protocol supports multiplexing and streaming
- All signals are handled correctly

close_reason:
Phase 1.2 core types and traits verification complete.

## Retrospective
- **What worked:** Systematic verification by examining source files, running tests, and checking against plan requirements. The grep-based search for secrecy usage was efficient.
- **What didn't:** Initial test run showed a failure, but investigation revealed it was an environment-specific issue (kernel keyring not available in container) rather than a code bug.
- **Surprise:** The secrecy crate is in Cargo.toml dependencies but not actually used anywhere in sigil-core. The plan marks it as complete, but the code uses Zeroizing directly instead of SecretString/SecretVec wrappers.
- **Reusable pattern:** For verification tasks, check (1) source code directly, (2) run tests, (3) run clippy, (4) check dependencies vs actual usage. The discrepancy between plan and implementation can be found by grepping for specific types.

title: sigil quickstart command: one-command onboarding flow
description:
The README and quickstart guide reference 'sigil quickstart' as a one-command setup, but this command is not explicitly tracked.

## What sigil quickstart should do:

1. Detect platform (Linux bwrap / macOS Seatbelt / WSL2)
2. Check prerequisites: bwrap availability, Rust, required system packages
3. sigil init: create vault, generate age keypair, prompt passphrase
4. Guide user through adding first secret (interactive)
5. sigil setup claude-code (or appropriate agent): install hooks
6. Run sigil doctor to verify setup
7. Print summary with next steps

## Implementation tasks:

2. Interactive prompts with colored output (no TUI dependency — use stdin/stdout)
3. Progress indicators for each step
4. Error handling with clear remediation for each failure mode
5. --agent flag to specify which agent to set up hooks for
6. Non-interactive mode: sigil quickstart --non-interactive --passphrase-file <file>
7. Verify demo.sh in docs/ matches the implemented quickstart flow
8. Update docs/quickstart.md to match actual sigil quickstart behavior

## Notes
- docs/demo.sh exists — review for the intended flow
- This is the entry point referenced in the README 3-command quickstart

title: Phase 2.4: Verify three daemon startup modes
description:
Verify on-demand, systemd, and launchd startup modes.

## On-demand startup (default):
- [ ] Lockfile coordination: /run/user/1000/sigil.lock
- [ ] Client checks if daemon running via socket probe
- [ ] If not running: acquire lockfile, fork daemon, wait for socket (max 5s)
- [ ] Race-safe: multiple clients result in exactly one daemon
- [ ] Daemon remains running after client disconnects

## systemd socket activation (Linux):
- [ ] sigil setup systemd installs ~/.config/systemd/user/sigil.socket and sigil.service
- [ ] Daemon receives socket fd via  (sd_listen_fds protocol)
- [ ] sd_notify(READY=1) after secrets loaded
- [ ] SocketMode=0600 in unit file

## launchd (macOS):
- [ ] sigil setup launchd installs ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.sigil.daemon.plist
- [ ] Daemon receives socket fd via launchd check-in API
- [ ] SockPathName uses /home/coding/.tmp/sigil.sock
- [ ] SockPathMode=384 (0600 octal)

## Idle timeout shutdown:
- [ ] Configurable idle timeout (default 30m)
- [ ] Daemon tracks last activity timestamp
- [ ] On timeout: graceful shutdown (zeroize, close socket, remove lockfile)
- [ ] idle_timeout = "never" disables timeout

## Tests:
- Kill daemon, run client command, verify auto-start
- Run sigil setup systemd, verify unit files installed
- On macOS: run sigil setup launchd, verify plist loaded
- Set idle_timeout=10s, wait, verify daemon shuts down

## Acceptance
- All three startup modes work correctly
- Idle timeout is configurable and functional

title: sigil-shamir: implement full SLIP39 Shamir Secret Sharing
description:
sigil-shamir/src/lib.rs is only 71 lines — it is a stub. The plan requires full SLIP39-compatible Shamir Secret Sharing for team vaults and recovery codes.

## Implementation required:

The plan calls for:
- SLIP39 implementation for Shamir's Secret Sharing
- Configurable threshold: M-of-N where M >= 2 and N <= 16
- Share verification: each share has a checksum to detect transcription errors
- Share rotation: replace a compromised share without changing the master key
- 20-word mnemonic encoding for recovery codes (SLIP39-style)
- 8 single-use recovery codes with used-code tracking in vault header

## Tasks:
1. Implement GF(256) polynomial arithmetic for Shamir shares
2. Implement SLIP39 word encoding/decoding (2048-word list, 20-word shares)
3. Share generation: split(secret, threshold, n_shares) → Vec<Share>
4. Share combination: combine(shares) → secret (with threshold check)
5. Checksum validation per share
6. Integrate with vault sealed mode: sigil init --shamir 3,5 flow
7. sigil unseal --share "word word word..." (multiple --share flags)
8. Recovery code generation at init: 8 single-use 20-word phrases
9. Used code tracking: store in vault header, check before accepting
10. Unit tests: roundtrip, wrong threshold, wrong shares, checksum detection

## Notes
- The sharks or vsss-rs crate may handle the GF(256) math
- Check Cargo.toml for existing dependencies before adding new ones
- sigil-vault/src/recovery.rs (2349 lines) may already have recovery code logic — review and integrate

- Verify session token exists in keyring: keyctl read

close_reason:
Phase 2.1 daemon hardening verified.

Created comprehensive runtime integration tests that verify all daemon hardening measures are properly implemented:

1. PR_SET_DUMPABLE=0 - Confirmed /proc/<pid>/status shows dumpable: 0
2. mlockall(MCL_CURRENT | MCL_FUTURE) - Confirmed memory locking logs
3. Kernel keyring session token - Confirmed token stored in kernel, not file
4. RLIMIT_CORE=0 - Confirmed /proc/<pid>/limits shows core size 0
5. Socket 0600 permissions - Confirmed socket has owner-only permissions

All tests pass.

## Retrospective
- **What worked:** The existing daemon implementation already had all the hardening measures in place. The task was primarily verification rather than implementation.
- **What didn't:** Initial test runs showed all tests passing immediately — the hardening was already complete from prior work.
- **Surprise:** The kernel keyring implementation was more complete than expected, including a fallback to file-based storage with proper 0400 permissions for non-Linux systems.
- **Reusable pattern:** Runtime integration tests that spawn a real daemon process and verify /proc filesystem state are an excellent way to validate security hardening that static code analysis cannot confirm.

- [ ] Client checks if daemon running via socket probe
- [ ] If not running: acquire lockfile, fork daemon, wait for socket (max 5s)
- [ ] Race-safe: multiple clients result in exactly one daemon
- [ ] Daemon remains running after client disconnects

- [ ] sigil setup systemd installs ~/.config/systemd/user/sigil.socket and sigil.service

- [ ] sd_notify(READY=1) after secrets loaded

- [ ] sigil setup launchd installs ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.sigil.daemon.plist
- [ ] Daemon receives socket fd via launchd check-in API
- [ ] SockPathName uses $TMPDIR/sigil.sock (macOS-specific path)
- [ ] SockPathMode=384 (0600 octal)

## Idle timeout shutdown:
- [ ] Configurable idle timeout (default 30m): [daemon] idle_timeout = "30m"
- [ ] Daemon tracks last activity timestamp
- [ ] On timeout: graceful shutdown (zeroize, close socket, remove lockfile)
- [ ] On-demand mode restarts automatically on next request
- [ ] idle_timeout = "never" disables timeout
